trajectory_summary: The call opened with a structurally weak lead — Debbie answered for Mike, disclosed low personal interest immediately, and framed herself as a referral conduit rather than a buyer. Bucky's responses were reactive throughout: he pitched the bill-swap program without first addressing Debbie's framing, introduced an unnecessary geographic tangent that raised legitimacy questions, and accepted her exit without anchoring Mike or creating a concrete next step. The call likely ends without a sit.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is unknown. Based on the transcript, the most probable outcome is no appointment booked — Debbie's exit ('let me think about it / send my husband an email') combined with Bucky's non-committal text offer ('text me back if you want') produces a soft callback with no locked time, no Mike included, and no curiosity created. This is a fragile-to-no-outcome result, not a booked sit.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: I kinda wanted to get a sense of what it costs because other people are asking about it. That I do own that property, but I wasn't particularly interested.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Bucky had a genuine qualifier signal — property owner, embedded in a social network discussing solar — and responded with a generic product pitch. The coaching priority is teaching Bucky to hear 'I wasn't particularly interested' as the entry point for a curiosity-creation move, not as a signal to pitch harder. This pattern recurred three times on this call and will recur on every low-intent lead Bucky works. Getting this move right changes the trajectory of the call type, not just this call.

key_moments.quote: No. This is Debbie. It's wife.
key_moments.stewart_read: Debbie answering for Mike is the structural origin of the call's biggest unresolved problem. The rep never asked whether Debbie or Mike is on the title, never confirmed who the decision-maker is, and never attempted to include Mike in any next step until Debbie herself raised him at 02:55. The verify.title_co_owners item was never completed.

key_moments.quote: I kinda wanted to get a sense of what it costs because other people are asking about it. That I do own that property, but I wasn't particularly interested.
key_moments.stewart_read: Debbie's referral-network signal — 'other people are asking about it' — is not covered by a current schema section (TBD gap; closest to analysis_directives.trust_building_micro_trades). This is not a personal solar motivation (verify.interest_in_solar does not apply here). However, it is a meaningful qualifier: Debbie owns the property, is embedded in a social network where solar is being discussed, and is doing research on behalf of others. A rep who recognized this could have reframed the conversation: 'So you're the one people come to with questions — let's make sure you have the real numbers, not just an estimator.' That reframe creates a reason for Debbie to engage that fits her actual posture.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. And then you know about the bill swap too?
key_moments.stewart_read: Bucky's bill-swap pitch is delivered before any verify items are completed — no address, no title confirmation, no bill, no roof age, nothing. The pitch is also feature-forward ('we replace your power bill with a solar bill') rather than curiosity-creating. The schema is explicit: setters should make customers curious to see their specific math, not explain the product. Bucky is selling solar to someone who said she wasn't interested, without any personalized anchor to make the pitch relevant.

key_moments.quote: because the the state did try to, extend the solar credit. Okay. And, I don't think that worked out real well, but I think the feds are the ones that cut off the solar credit for residential.
key_moments.stewart_read: Debbie is operating on incomplete or incorrect information about the federal tax credit. The schema's tax_credit_disclosure note directly applies: Ion can no longer guarantee the tax credit on purchased systems, but installation prior to year-end may still allow the customer to claim it, and purchase options without the credit exist. Bucky's response ('Okay. So the the concern there. Yeah. I mean, we can...') trails off without correcting the misinformation or using it as a curiosity-creation bridge. This was a live opening: 'Actually, there's still a window — that's exactly what our specialist would walk you through. Want to get on the calendar before that window closes?'
